1: Psalm 105 opens a matched pair with Ps 106 — two great historical retrospectives placed side by side to form a diptych: YHWH's unbroken faithfulness (105) alongside Israel's unbroken unfaithfulness (106). Both open and close with "Praise the LORD" (hallĕlû-yāh). Psalms 105:1-15 is also found in 1 Chronicles 16:8-22, the prayer David composed when the ark was brought to Jerusalem — its liturgical use at that defining moment suggests it became a foundational recitation of covenant history. The opening summons ("Give thanks to the LORD, call on his name") combines two primary acts of covenant worship: tôdāh (thanksgiving) and qārāʾ (calling on the name, invoking his presence). "Make known his deeds among the peoples" — the horizontal dimension of praise: YHWH's acts in history are not private information but public testimony.
2: "Sing to him, sing praises to him; tell of all his wondrous works" — the threefold summons (šîrû, zammĕrû, śîḥû) covers the three modes of musical praise: singing (šîr), playing/singing (zimmer), and telling/meditating aloud (śîaḥ). "His wondrous works (niplĕʾôtāyw)" — the standard term for the mighty acts of God in history, especially the plagues and Exodus. In Second Temple Judaism, retelling the niplĕʾôt was an act of covenant loyalty — children were taught to recite them (Psalms 78:4; Deuteronomy 6:20-25), and the Passover Haggadah structures this recitation as the central act of remembrance.
3: "Glory in his holy name; let the hearts of those who seek the LORD rejoice" — "glory in his name (hithallĕlû bĕšēm qāḏšô)" is the exact phrase Paul quotes in 1 Corinthians 1:31 and 2 Corinthians 10:17 (via Jeremiah 9:24): "Let the one who boasts, boast in the Lord." The name (šēm) is the covenantal self-disclosure of YHWH — to glory in the name is to find one's identity and status in YHWH's character rather than in one's own achievements. "Those who seek the LORD (mĕbaqqĕšê YHWH)" — the covenantal category of those who actively orient themselves toward divine presence (cf. Amos 5:4-6: "Seek me and live").
4: "Seek the LORD and his strength; seek his presence (pānāyw) continually" — the double summons to seek both strength (ʿuz) and face (pānîm). In the covenantal framework, "seeking the face" of the king was the formal act of accessing royal presence and protection (cf. 2 Samuel 14:24, 28, 32; 21:1 where "seeking the face of YHWH" is an oracular inquiry). The continuous, repeated (tāmîd) nature of the seeking distinguishes covenant relationship from transactional worship. The logic of the verse: divine strength is accessed through divine presence, not through independent human effort.
5: "Remember the wondrous works he has done, his miracles, and the judgments he pronounced" — the act of remembering (zākar) is not passive recollection but active appropriation of past divine acts as present reality. In the Passover liturgy (Deuteronomy 16:3; Exodus 12:14), remembrance creates continuity between the original Exodus participants and every subsequent generation: "You shall remember that you were a slave in Egypt." The three-part formula — wondrous works (niplĕʾôtāyw), miracles (môpĕtāyw), and judgments (mišpĕṭê-pîw) — covers the full range of divine action in history: creation wonders, covenant-confirming signs, and legal pronouncements.
6: "O seed of Abraham, his servant, O children of Jacob, his chosen ones" — the address to the covenant community draws the two central patriarchal lineages into one: Abraham as ʿebed (servant, the same title applied to Moses and David in highest honor — Deuteronomy 34:5; 2 Samuel 3:18) and Jacob as bĕḥîrāyw (chosen ones). The "chosen" language (bāḥar) is the key covenantal election verb (Deuteronomy 7:6: "the LORD your God has chosen you out of all the peoples"). Both titles — servant and chosen — define identity not through achievement but through relationship: you are who you are because of what YHWH did in choosing your ancestors.
7: "He is the LORD our God; his judgments are in all the earth" — the pivot from summons (Psalms 105:1-6) to theological claim. "The LORD our God" (YHWH ʾĕlōhênû) is the standard covenant confessional formula (Deuteronomy 6:4 — the Shema begins "YHWH our God, YHWH is one"). "His judgments (mišpāṭāyw) are in all the earth" extends the claim from Israel's local deity to the God of universal jurisdiction. In the ANE theological framework, each people had their own god whose authority was limited to their territory. Ps 105 asserts YHWH's authority is geographically unlimited — his mišpāṭîm govern all nations.
8: "He has remembered his covenant forever, the word he commanded, for a thousand generations" — "a thousand generations" (ʾelep dôr) is the hyperbolic expression of permanence (Deuteronomy 7:9 uses the same phrase). The verse asserts the absolute durability of the Abrahamic covenant. "The word he commanded" — the covenant has the force of divine command (ṣāwāh), not merely a mutual agreement. "He has remembered (zākar)" — the same verb used of the Noah covenant (Genesis 8:1 — "God remembered Noah") and the Exodus covenant (Exodus 2:24 — "God remembered his covenant with Abraham"). Divine remembering is always followed by divine acting.
9: "The covenant he made with Abraham, the oath he swore to Isaac" — two of the three forms in which the Abrahamic covenant was communicated: covenant (bĕrît) with Abraham (Gen 15; 17) and oath (šĕbûʿāh) to Isaac (Genesis 26:3-5). The distinction between covenant and oath is significant: a covenant could theoretically be renegotiated; an oath invoked YHWH's own existence as guarantee. The oath-character of the Abrahamic promise is emphasized in Hebrews 6:13-18 (the author of Hebrews grounds Christian assurance in the impossibility of divine oath-breaking).
10: "He confirmed it to Jacob as a statute, to Israel as an everlasting covenant" — the Abrahamic covenant's extension to Jacob is presented as both ḥōq (statute, binding regulation, similar to a royal decree) and bĕrît ʿôlām (everlasting covenant). Bĕrît ʿôlām is the highest designation for covenant permanence in the Hebrew Bible — it applies also to the Noahic covenant (Genesis 9:16), the Sabbath (Exodus 31:16), and the Davidic covenant (2 Samuel 23:5). The use of "Jacob" and "Israel" as parallel names for the same person (both given by God — Genesis 32:28; Psalms 35:10) suggests the totality of the patriarch's identity is included in the covenant.
11: "Saying, 'To you I will give the land of Canaan as your allotted inheritance (ḥeḇel naḥălat-kem)'" — the land promise is the concrete content of the covenant. Ḥeḇel (measuring line, allotment) is the technical term for the apportioning of land by lot — each tribe received its ḥeḇel in the settlement (Joshua 17:5, 14). Naḥălāh (inheritance, patrimony) emphasizes that the land is not conquered real estate but inherited family property given by divine grant. In Levitical law, inherited land could not be permanently sold (Leviticus 25:23-24) because "the land is mine; you are but aliens and strangers resident with me" — YHWH owns the land and Israel holds it as tenants.
12: "When they were but a few people in number, very few, and strangers (gērîm) in it" — the historical moment of the promise is emphasized: the covenant was made when Israel was numerically insignificant and legally without status (gērîm = resident aliens, without inheritance rights in the land). This is the theology of divine election against all odds: the promise was not made to a nation already established but to wandering strangers with no claim to the land. The covenant creates the nation, not the reverse. Deuteronomy 7:7-8 makes this explicit: "The LORD did not choose you because you were more numerous than other peoples... but because the LORD loves you."
13: "Wandering from nation to nation, from one kingdom to another people" — the patriarchal narrative in summary: Abraham moved from Ur to Haran to Canaan to Egypt; Isaac moved within Canaan among the Philistines; Jacob moved from Canaan to Paddan-Aram and back, then to Egypt. The wandering (yithaʿlĕkû) from nation to nation captures the rootlessness of the pre-settlement period. In the covenant's logic, this period of vulnerability serves to demonstrate that the patriarchs' safety was entirely dependent on YHWH's protection, not on political power or territorial control.
14: "He allowed no one to oppress them; he rebuked kings on their account" — the specific incidents recalled: the rebuke of Pharaoh (Genesis 12:17-20, where God afflicted Pharaoh's household after Abraham's deception about Sarah) and Abimelech of Gerar (Genesis 20:3-7, where God appeared to Abimelech in a dream with a death warning: "You are as good as dead"). In both cases, the wandering aliens (with no legal protection) were protected by direct divine intervention against royal power. The verb "rebuked (wĕyôkaḥ)" suggests forensic correction — a formal, authoritative reprimand.
15: "'Do not touch my anointed ones; do not harm my prophets'" — the patriarchs are described with two exalted titles: mĕšîḥāyw (anointed ones) and nĕbîʾāyw (prophets). Neither term is applied to the patriarchs in Genesis itself — this is the Psalter's interpretive move. The "anointed" designation anticipates the priestly and royal anointing rituals; applied to the patriarchs it means they were consecrated to God's service. "Prophets" — Abraham is explicitly called a prophet in Genesis 20:7 (the Abimelech narrative, just recalled in Psalms 105:14). The warning "do not touch" (ʾal-tiggĕʿû) is the strongest possible prohibition.
16: "He called down a famine on the land and broke every staff of bread (maṭṭēh leḥem)" — the Joseph narrative begins (Psalms 105:16-22). "Broke every staff of bread" — maṭṭēh leḥem (staff of bread) is an idiom for the food supply as a support beam for life (cf. Leviticus 26:26; Ezekiel 4:16; Psalms 5:16; Psalms 14:13 — all use "breaking the staff of bread" as judgment). The famine is presented as YHWH's deliberate action ("he called down"), not a natural disaster — this frames the Joseph story theologically: YHWH orchestrated the famine to accomplish the covenant purpose of bringing Israel to Egypt and then delivering them from it.
17: "He sent a man before them — Joseph, sold as a slave" — the active subject is YHWH: "he sent (šālaḥ)." Joseph's being sold by his brothers (Genesis 37:28) is reinterpreted as divine sending. The same language appears in Genesis 45:5, 7, 8 where Joseph himself twice says "God sent me before you" and "God sent me ahead of you." The psalm draws on Joseph's own mature theological interpretation of his suffering. "A man before them" — a person positioned ahead in time to prepare the way, like a vanguard. The providential reversal — sold as a slave, becomes the deliverer — is the psalm's paradigm case of how YHWH works through human wickedness to fulfill covenant purposes.
18: "His feet were hurt with shackles; his neck was put in an iron collar (barzĕl bāʾāh napšô)" — the physical suffering of Joseph in Egyptian captivity. Barzĕl bāʾāh napšô is a grammatically difficult phrase: literally "iron entered into his soul/throat." Possible readings: (1) the iron collar pressed against his throat; (2) the iron shackles afflicted his whole being (nepeš as the seat of personal identity). The point is that the "sending" of Psalms 105:17 involved real physical suffering — the providential story should not be read as painless. Joseph endured years of physical hardship before the reversal.
19: "Until his prediction came true (ʿad-ʿēt bōʾ-dĕbārô) — the word of the LORD tested him (ʾimrat YHWH ṣĕrāpātû)" — "the word of the LORD tested him (ṣārap)" — ṣārap is the verb for smelting/refining metal to test its purity (Proverbs 17:3; Psalms 12:6; Psalms 18:30). Joseph was "refined" by his suffering. "His prediction (dĕbārô)" — the dreams of Genesis 37:5-11, which Joseph told his brothers and father. These dreams were the divine word that had to be tested through the years of slavery and imprisonment before they could be fulfilled. The verb "came true (bōʾ)" — literally "came," the same word used for the arrival of the prophetic word (cf. Isaiah 44:26; Ezekiel 33:33).
20: "The king sent and released him; the ruler of peoples set him free" — the passive form "sent" (šālaḥ) mirrors Psalms 105:17: just as YHWH "sent" Joseph to Egypt as a slave, Pharaoh "sent" to release him. The chiastic structure reinforces the providential irony. "Ruler of peoples (mōšēl ʿammîm)" — Pharaoh's universal title in the Egyptian world-view. The historical background: Joseph was released from prison because his God-given ability to interpret dreams was exactly what Pharaoh needed (Genesis 41:14-16). The psalm compresses the narrative but preserves the theological point: Pharaoh's action was instrumentalized by YHWH's purpose.
21: "He made him lord of his household, and ruler over all his possessions" — Genesis 41:41-44 records Pharaoh's appointment of Joseph: "I hereby put you in charge of the whole land of Egypt... without your word no one will lift hand or foot in all Egypt." The psalm summarizes this with the household/bêt vocabulary — Joseph administered Pharaoh's "house" (bêtô), which in Egyptian administrative parlance designated the royal household and its resources. The appointment echoes and reverses the beginning of the Joseph narrative: Potiphar put Joseph "in charge of his household" (Genesis 39:4) — the same language, now scaled to national level.
22: "To instruct his officials as he saw fit and teach his elders wisdom" — Joseph's authority extended to education and governance: he could "bind (le-ʾsōr)" his officials according to his will and "teach wisdom (yĕḥakkēm)" to the elders. The wisdom dimension is significant: in the Egyptian context, wisdom (ḥokmāh) was both practical administration and cosmic insight. The ability to interpret dreams (Gen 40-41) was the demonstration of divine wisdom, and this same wisdom was now applied to governance. Joseph became an administrator who operated from divine insight — a model later applied to Solomon and, in Wisdom literature, to the sage who serves in royal courts.
23: "Then Israel came to Egypt; Jacob lived as an alien (gār) in the land of Ham" — the descent to Egypt, which the psalm has been building toward through the Joseph narrative (Psalms 105:16-22), is summarized in a single verse. "Land of Ham" is the Psalter's poetic term for Egypt (cf. Psalms 78:51; Psalms 106:22) — Ham as the father of Mizraim (Egypt) in Genesis 10:6. "Israel" and "Jacob" used in parallel (as in Psalms 105:10) — the full national identity is present in the patriarch. The verb "lived as an alien (gār)" returns to the theme of Psalms 105:12 (the patriarchs as gērîm) — Israel's time in Egypt was an extended sojourn in alien status, fulfilling the prediction of Genesis 15:13.
24: "The LORD made his people very fruitful; he made them too numerous for their foes" — the fulfillment of the Abrahamic blessing (Genesis 12:2; Psalms 17:6; Psalms 22:17) happened in Egypt. The verb "made very fruitful (wayyipreh)" echoes Genesis 1:28 (the creation mandate "be fruitful and multiply") and specifically Genesis 47:27 ("Israel settled in Egypt... they acquired property there and were fruitful and increased greatly in number"). This is the paradox that creates the Exodus: YHWH's covenant-fulfilling blessing of population growth is precisely what triggers Egyptian hostility — "the Israelites were exceedingly fruitful... the land was filled with them" (Exodus 1:7) causes Pharaoh's fear.
25: "Whose heart he turned to hate his people, to scheme against his servants" — this theologically loaded verse asserts that YHWH turned (hāpak) the Egyptian heart to hatred. This is the hardening tradition: as in Exodus 4:21; Psalms 9:12; Psalms 10:1, 20, 27 (YHWH hardening Pharaoh's heart), the psalm places divine sovereignty behind the oppression. The theological problem this raises (why would YHWH incite hatred of his own people?) is resolved in the covenant logic: the oppression creates the conditions for the great deliverance that will demonstrate YHWH's power to Egypt and the world (Exodus 9:16 — "I raised you up for this purpose, to show you my power and that my name might be proclaimed in all the earth").
26: "He sent Moses his servant, Aaron, whom he had chosen" — exactly as YHWH "sent" Joseph (Psalms 105:17), he now "sends" Moses. The parallel structure of the psalm is deliberate: each deliverance comes through a divine sending of a person. Moses is called "his servant" (ʿabdô) — the same title as Abraham in Psalms 105:6 and the highest honor designation. Aaron is "whom he had chosen (bāḥar)" — the election verb from Psalms 105:6. The doubling of Moses and Aaron reflects Exodus 4:14-16 where YHWH designates Aaron as Moses's "mouth" and spokesman, addressing Moses's reluctance to speak. The sending structure of Ps 105 creates a pattern: YHWH solves each crisis through a chosen agent sent ahead.
27: "They performed his signs among them, his wonders (môpĕtāyw) in the land of Ham" — "signs and wonders" (ʾôtôt ûmôpĕtîm) is the technical phrase for the Exodus miracles (Deuteronomy 4:34; Psalms 6:22; Psalms 7:19; Psalms 26:8; Psalms 34:11; Psalms 78:43). The signs (ʾôtôt) have a communicative function — they are signifiers that point to YHWH's power. The wonders (môpĕtîm) are the portents that cause awe and signal divine action. The "land of Ham" (Egypt) as the theater of these signs is significant: YHWH's power is demonstrated not in Israel's own territory but in the heart of the world's dominant empire.
28: "He sent darkness and made it dark; and they did not rebel against his words" — the plague order in Ps 105 differs from Exod 7-12 and Ps 78. Here the plagues begin with darkness (plague 9 in Exodus). The last half of the verse, "they did not rebel against his words," is grammatically ambiguous — does "they" refer to Moses and Aaron (who obeyed, unlike later), or to the Egyptians (who did not resist the darkness)? Most translations follow the former reading. The psalm is concerned with the plagues as demonstrations of divine word-power: each plague is the execution of YHWH's spoken command.
29: "He turned their waters into blood and caused their fish to die" — plague 1 (Exodus 7:17-25). The Nile was the basis of Egyptian civilization — its annual inundation deposited the silt that made Egypt's agriculture possible. It was also sacred: the Nile was deified as Hapy, the god of inundation and fertility. Turning the Nile to blood was simultaneously a practical disaster (undrinkable water, dead fish as food source) and a theological judgment against the divine river. The fish dying was economically significant — fish was a major food source for Egypt (Numbers 11:5 — the Israelites remembered "the fish we ate in Egypt at no cost").
30: "Their land swarmed with frogs, even in the bedchambers of their kings" — plague 2 (Exodus 8:1-15). The frog (ṣĕpardēaʿ) was associated with Heqet, the Egyptian frog-goddess of fertility who assisted in childbirth. The infestation of frogs in the most private royal spaces (bedchambers) was both a practical horror and a theological statement: YHWH's power penetrates to the most protected spaces. The phrase "in the bedchambers of their kings" emphasizes that Egyptian royal protection was no barrier against the divine plague. The Revelation 16:13 image of three frog-like unclean spirits from the dragon, beast, and false prophet echoes this plague imagery.
31: "He spoke, and swarms of flies came, and gnats throughout their country" — plagues 3 (kinnîm, gnats/lice, Exodus 8:16-19) and 4 (ʿārōb, swarms/flies, Exodus 8:20-32) are combined in a single verse. The gnats were significant because the Egyptian magicians could not replicate them and said "This is the finger of God" (Exodus 8:19) — the first acknowledged defeat of the Egyptian court magicians. The swarms of flies were the first plague that specifically excluded Goshen (where Israel lived), demonstrating YHWH's selective application of judgment.
32: "He gave them hail for rain, and lightning throughout their land" — plague 7 (Exodus 9:13-35, hail). Exodus 9:23-24 describes it as the most severe hailstorm "in all the land of Egypt since it had become a nation." The plague was accompanied by fire (lightning) running along the ground — combining the natural disaster of hail with the supernatural element of fire. The hail destroyed the crops that were in growth — flax and barley (Exodus 9:31-32) but not wheat (which came later, explaining why Pharaoh relented and then hardened again). The psalm connects hail and lightning as manifestations of YHWH's storm-warrior theophany.
33: "He struck down their vines and their fig trees and shattered the trees of their country" — still the hail plague (Exodus 9:25: "it beat down every plant in the field and stripped every tree"). Vines and fig trees as paradigmatic food sources — their destruction represents total agricultural devastation. In Israel's covenant theology, sitting "under your vine and fig tree" (1 Kings 4:25; Micah 4:4; Zechariah 3:10) was the image of covenant prosperity and peace. The destruction of vine and fig tree was the inversion of this blessing — the covenant curse applied to Egypt.
34: "He spoke, and locusts came, and young locusts without number (wĕyeleq wĕʾên mispār)" — plague 8 (Exodus 10:1-20, locusts). "He spoke" (ʾāmar) — the plague comes through divine speech-command, reinforcing the word-power theology of the psalm. The young locusts (yeleq — a specific stage of locust development, also translated "cricket" or "locust nymph") in addition to the adult locusts (ʾarbeh) emphasize the completeness of the infestation. Exodus 10:14 describes the locust swarm as unprecedented: "never before had there been such a locust plague on Egypt, nor would there ever be again." Joel 1-2 would later use locust imagery for eschatological judgment.
35: "They devoured all the vegetation in their land and ate up the fruit of their ground" — the locust plague completed what the hail had left: all crops and vegetation were consumed. Exodus 10:15 says the locusts "covered all the ground until it was black. They devoured all that was left after the hail — everything growing in the fields and the fruit on the trees. Nothing green remained on tree or plant in all the land of Egypt." The total stripping of Egypt's agricultural base represents the inversion of the land's natural fertility — the Egypt that was "like the garden of the LORD, like the land of Egypt" (Genesis 13:10) became desolate.
36: "He also struck down all the firstborn in their land, the firstfruits of all their manhood" — plague 10 (Exodus 12:29-30). "Firstborn" (bĕkôr) and "firstfruits of all their manhood (rēʾšît kol-ʾônām)" are parallel terms — the firstborn as the firstfruits of human reproductive strength. In ANE culture, the firstborn son carried the family name, inheritance, and honor. The simultaneous death of every Egyptian firstborn from the royal house to the prisoner was a social catastrophe beyond any of the preceding plagues. Exodus 4:22-23 had established the theological logic: "Israel is my firstborn son... let my son go... or I will kill your firstborn son."
37: "He led Israel out with silver and gold, and not one among his tribes stumbled" — Exodus 12:35-36: the Israelites asked the Egyptians for silver and gold objects, and "the LORD had made the Egyptians favorably disposed toward the people, and they gave them what they asked for; so they plundered the Egyptians." The psalm describes this as Israel going out "laden with silver and gold" — the plundering of the oppressors as a form of back-payment for centuries of slave labor. "Not one stumbled (wĕʾên bôkēš bišbāṭāyw)" — the entire nation, after generations of slavery, departed in an organized, healthy procession. The YHWH who promised to bring them out "with great wealth" (Genesis 15:14) fulfilled his word.
38: "Egypt was glad when they left, for the dread of Israel had fallen on them" — Exodus 12:33 records that "the Egyptians urged the people to hurry and leave the country." After ten plagues, Egypt wanted to be rid of Israel. The "dread (paḥad)" of Israel is the covenantal terror that YHWH promised to send ahead of his people (Exodus 23:27-28; Deuteronomy 2:25; Psalms 11:25). In the military context, this is the divine-warrior tradition: YHWH goes before his people and causes their enemies' hearts to melt (Joshua 2:9-11 — Rahab reports that "a great fear of you has fallen on us").
39: "He spread a cloud for covering, and fire to give light at night" — the wilderness provision (Psalms 105:39-41). The pillar of cloud and fire (Exodus 13:21-22; Psalms 14:19-24) served multiple functions: guidance (leading), protection (covering from the desert sun and protecting the rear at the sea crossing), and communication (the divine presence appearing in the cloud, Exodus 33:9-10; Numbers 12:5). The cloud as "covering (sēk)" suggests a protective canopy — shielding Israel from the Sinai sun. In Second Temple interpretation, the cloud became associated with the Šĕkînāh, the divine dwelling presence.
40: "They asked, and he brought quail, and satisfied them with bread from heaven (leḥem šāmayim)" — the quail and manna (Exod 16; Num 11). The "asking" in the psalm is the Israelites' petition for food — in Numbers 11, this asking is laden with ingratitude and complaint ("who will give us meat to eat?"). The psalm' summarizes the provision without the complaint — a different rhetorical emphasis than Ps 78 (which will dwell on the sin) or Ps 106 (which will recall the rebellion). "Bread from heaven (leḥem šāmayim)" — the phrase Jesus picks up in John 6:31-35, where he identifies himself as the true bread from heaven that the manna only anticipated.
41: "He opened the rock, and water gushed out; it flowed like a river through the desert" — the water from the rock (Exodus 17:1-7 at Horeb/Meribah; Numbers 20:1-13 at Meribah-Kadesh). "It flowed like a river (nāhār) through the desert" — an abundant, flowing river in the arid Sinai wilderness. Paul reads this providential provision christologically in 1 Corinthians 10:4: "they drank from the spiritual rock that accompanied them, and that rock was Christ." The rock that produces water in the wilderness becomes a type: divine provision through an unexpected source.
42: "For he remembered his holy promise to Abraham his servant" — the summarizing theological principle of the entire historical survey. Everything recounted in Psalms 105:7-41 (the patriarchal protection, the Joseph providential reversal, the Exodus plagues, the wilderness provision) had a single divine motivation: YHWH remembered (zākar) his promise (dĕbar-qāḏšô, his holy word) to Abraham. The covenant promise of Genesis 12:1-3; 15; 17; Psalms 22:15-18 was the engine driving every subsequent divine act. "Abraham his servant" — the title repeats from Psalms 105:6 and Psalms 105:26, 42 form a bracket: the psalm opened by addressing "seed of Abraham, his servant" (Psalms 105:6) and now closes by naming the patriarch himself.
43: "He led out his people with rejoicing, his chosen ones with shouts of joy" — the Exodus is experienced as festive celebration (bĕśāśôn = with joy, exultation; bĕrinnāh = with shouts/singing). This matches the Song of Moses (Exodus 15:1-21) — the Exodus was received with singing and dancing at the sea. The "chosen ones (bĕḥîrāyw)" return to the election theme of Psalms 105:6 — the people who began the psalm as the "chosen" of God have now been rescued in fulfillment of the covenant. The Exodus as joyful procession contrasts with Ps 106's account of the same events, where the dominant tone is failure and judgment.
44: "He gave them the lands of the nations; they took possession of what other peoples had worked for" — the gift of the land (Josh 11-21). Deuteronomy 6:10-11 anticipates this: "The LORD your God will bring you into the land... large, flourishing cities you did not build, houses filled with all kinds of good things you did not provide, wells you did not dig, vineyards and olive groves you did not plant." The psalm echoes this Deuteronomic theology of the land as inheritance, not conquest achievement. The nations' cities, fields, and vineyards were given by YHWH for Israel to inhabit — a radical gift economy that emphasized divine generosity.
45: "So that they might keep his precepts (ḥuqqāyw) and observe his laws (tôrōtāyw). Praise the LORD (hallĕlû-yāh)!" — the purpose clause that closes the entire historical survey. The entire sequence — covenant, patriarchs, Joseph, plagues, Exodus, wilderness, land — was aimed at this: Israel keeping Torah. The gift of the land was not an end in itself but the means of covenant life, the space in which obedience to YHWH's precepts was possible. The Deuteronomic logic: "Do this and live" (Deuteronomy 30:15-20). The closing Hallĕlû-yāh mirrors the opening call of Psalms 105:1 and will open Ps 106 — the linked pair of psalms frames both grace and law within the praise of the covenant God.
